codeExamples.explanation: Query keys uniquely identify cached data. staleTime controls freshness. isLoading is true only on first load, while isFetching is true during any fetch (including background refetches).

codeExamples.title: Mutations and Cache Invalidation
codeExamples.explanation: Mutations handle writes. After success, invalidateQueries triggers a refetch of related data, or setQueryData updates the cache directly for instant UI updates.

codeExamples.title: Optimistic Updates
codeExamples.explanation: Optimistic updates show changes instantly. onMutate saves a snapshot and updates the cache. onError rolls back. onSettled refetches to ensure server and client are in sync.

notes: React Query replaces most useEffect data fetching patterns - it handles loading, errors, caching, and race conditions automatically
Query keys should include all variables that affect the fetched data (filters, pagination, IDs)
staleTime vs gcTime: staleTime controls when data is considered fresh (no refetch). gcTime controls when inactive cached data is garbage collected
React Query DevTools (ReactQueryDevtools) are essential for debugging - they show all cached queries, their status, and data
followUps: How do you implement infinite scroll / pagination with React Query?
What is the difference between staleTime and gcTime?
How does React Query handle race conditions compared to raw useEffect?
interviewTips: Explain why React Query is better than raw useEffect for data fetching: automatic caching, race condition handling, retry, and background refetch
The optimistic update pattern is a common interview topic - know the onMutate -> onError -> onSettled flow
Show understanding of query key design: include all parameters that affect the response

content.question: What are common React anti-patterns? How do you identify and fix them?
content.answer:
React anti-patterns are recurring code patterns that lead to bugs, poor performance, or unmaintainable code. Identifying and fixing them is a key skill that distinguishes experienced React developers.

State-related anti-patterns include: derived state that duplicates source data (compute it instead), unnecessary global state (most state should be local), and state that should be in the URL (filters, pagination, selected tabs). Component structure anti-patterns include: god components that do everything (break them up), prop drilling through many levels (use context or composition), and premature abstraction (don't extract components until you see clear patterns).

Performance anti-patterns include: creating objects and functions inside render (unstable references), unnecessary useEffect for derived values (compute during render), fetching data in useEffect without a library (missing caching, race conditions, error handling), and over-memoizing everything (adds complexity without measurable benefit).

The most insidious anti-pattern is using useEffect as a state synchronization mechanism. If you find yourself using useEffect to update state based on other state or props, you almost always can compute that value directly during rendering or handle it in the event handler that triggered the change.

codeExamples.title: Unnecessary useEffect (Most Common Anti-Pattern)
codeExamples.explanation: Most useEffect calls for state synchronization can be replaced by computing during render, handling in event handlers, or using the key prop for resets. useEffect should be reserved for synchronizing with external systems.

codeExamples.title: Component Structure Anti-Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: God components are hard to maintain and test. Prop drilling can often be solved with composition before reaching for context. Components defined inside render functions are recreated every render, causing full remounts.

codeExamples.title: State and Data Anti-Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Derived state causes unnecessary complexity and sync bugs. URL state makes filters bookmarkable and shareable. State mutations cause silent bugs because React cannot detect changes to the same reference.

notes: The React docs explicitly say: 'You might not need an effect' - this is the single most common anti-pattern in React codebases
If you need useEffect to synchronize two pieces of state, it is almost always a sign you should restructure your code
Components defined inside render functions are a subtle but severe performance bug - they cause child remounting on every render
State in URLs (search params) is better than React state for filters, pagination, and tabs because it survives refreshes and is shareable
followUps: How do you refactor a god component into smaller pieces?
What is the difference between necessary and unnecessary useEffect calls?
interviewTips: Showing awareness of anti-patterns demonstrates real-world experience - interviewers value this highly
Know the 'You Might Not Need an Effect' principle from the React docs - it is the most impactful refactoring guideline
Discuss anti-patterns you have actually encountered and fixed in your projects - concrete examples are more convincing than theory
